title: Asking and Answering Questions
introBody: Great readers ask questions to understand what they read. Today we will learn how to ask and answer questions about a story by looking at what the text says. Let's dive in!
introImage: A cartoon student sitting at a desk surrounded by floating question marks and exclamation points, eagerly reading a thick adventure book.
teachHeading: Using the Text to Find Answers
teachBody: When someone asks a question about a story, you can find the answer by referring to the text. This means going back to the story and finding the exact part that answers the question. The text is your best friend!
teachImage: A cartoon hand using a highlighter to mark important sentences in an open book, with arrows pointing from a question mark to the highlighted answer.
exampleBody: Read this: The explorer pushed through the thick jungle vines. She discovered a hidden waterfall behind the tallest tree. Question: What did the explorer discover? Answer: A hidden waterfall! We found the answer right in the second sentence.
exampleImage: A brave cartoon girl explorer with a backpack and hat pushing through green jungle vines to discover a sparkling blue waterfall.
tipBody: When you are not sure of an answer, go back and reread the part of the story that the question is about. Look for key words from the question in the text. This is called referring to the text!
tipImage: A cartoon child reading a book with a curved arrow going from a question mark back to a highlighted paragraph in the book.
practiceBody: You have learned how to find answers right in the text! Now it is time to practice. Read each story carefully and refer back to the text to find your answers. Let's go!
practiceImage: A confident cartoon student giving a thumbs up while standing next to a podium with a gold trophy that says Reading Champ.

title: Finding the Message or Moral
introHeading: What Is the Story Trying to Teach You?
introBody: Stories like fables, folktales, and myths often have a special lesson hiding inside them. Today we will learn how to figure out the central message, lesson, or moral of a story. Get ready to become a message detective!
introImage: A cartoon detective child with a magnifying glass examining a large open storybook with glowing words like "lesson" and "moral" floating above the pages.
teachHeading: The Central Message Is the Big Lesson
teachBody: The central message is the big idea or lesson the author wants you to learn. To find it, think about what the characters do, what happens to them, and what they learn by the end. The moral is usually something that can help you in real life too!
teachImage: A cartoon lightbulb glowing above an open book of fables, with small scenes from "The Tortoise and the Hare" and "The Boy Who Cried Wolf" on each page.
exampleBody: Read this: The ant worked hard all summer storing food. The grasshopper played and danced instead. When winter came, the ant had plenty to eat, but the grasshopper had nothing. Moral: Hard work pays off! We can tell because the ant's hard work helped him survive winter.
exampleImage: A cartoon ant happily carrying a piece of grain into a cozy underground home stocked with food, while snow falls outside and a shivering grasshopper peeks in.
tipHeading: Ask Yourself: What Did the Character Learn?
tipBody: After you finish a story, ask yourself what the main character learned. Their lesson is usually the story's central message. If a character changes from selfish to kind, the message might be about sharing!
tipImage: A cartoon child sitting cross-legged with a thought bubble showing a character's journey from making a mistake to learning a lesson, connected by an arrow.
practiceBody: Now you know how to find the central message in stories! Read each story and think about what the characters learn. Use those clues to find the moral. You can do this!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing proudly on a stage holding a scroll that reads "I found the message!" with an audience of smiling animal characters clapping.

title: Characters and Their Actions
introBody: Characters are the people or animals in a story. What they do and say moves the story forward. Today we will learn how to describe characters and explain how their actions shape what happens next!
introImage: A lineup of five diverse cartoon characters standing on a stage: a brave knight, a curious scientist, a friendly dragon, a shy artist, and a bold pirate captain.
teachBody: Every time a character makes a choice or does something, it causes the next event in the story. If a character decides to open a mysterious door, that action leads to whatever happens next. Characters' traits like bravery, kindness, or curiosity explain why they act the way they do.
teachImage: A cartoon flowchart showing a character deciding to climb a beanstalk, leading to discovering a giant's castle, leading to a chase scene, all connected by arrows.
exampleHeading: Watch How Actions Change the Story!
exampleBody: Read this: Maya was brave and curious. She found a treasure map in her attic and decided to follow it. Her bravery led her deep into the forest where she discovered an old chest full of gold coins. Maya's curiosity and bravery caused her to find the treasure!
exampleImage: A brave cartoon girl with a ponytail holding a treasure map, walking confidently into a sun-dappled forest with a trail leading to a glowing treasure chest.
tipBody: When you read, pay attention to what characters say, do, and feel. These clues tell you about their traits. Then notice how each action leads to the next event. It is like a chain of dominoes!
tipImage: A row of colorful cartoon dominoes falling in sequence, each one labeled with a character action like "shared," "helped," "explored," and "solved."
practiceBody: You are ready to become a character expert! Read each story and pay close attention to what the characters do. Think about how their actions cause the events in the story. Let's go!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a director's beret and holding a clapperboard that says "Character Action!" while cartoon characters act out scenes on a small stage.

title: Words That Mean More Than You Think
introBody: Sometimes words in a story mean exactly what they say, and sometimes they mean something totally different! Today we will learn to tell the difference between literal and nonliteral language. It is like having a secret decoder ring for reading!
introImage: A cartoon child holding a glowing decoder ring up to a book page where some words are shown in plain text and others shimmer with magical sparkles.
teachBody: Literal language means exactly what it says. "The cat sat on the mat" is literal. Nonliteral language uses words in a fun or creative way. "It's raining cats and dogs" does not mean animals are falling from the sky. It means it is raining very hard!
teachImage: A split-screen cartoon: on the left, a real cat sitting on a mat labeled "Literal." On the right, cartoon cats and dogs falling from clouds with a big X through it, labeled "Nonliteral."
exampleBody: Read this: Sam had butterflies in his stomach before the spelling bee. Does Sam really have butterflies inside him? No way! This is nonliteral language. It means Sam felt nervous and excited. The words paint a picture of how he felt.
exampleImage: A cartoon boy standing at a spelling bee podium with a thought bubble showing colorful butterflies fluttering around, representing his nervous but excited feeling.
tipBody: When you read an interesting phrase, ask yourself if it makes sense if taken word for word. If someone says "I'm over the moon," are they really floating in space? Nope! It means they are super happy. That is your clue it is nonliteral!
tipImage: A cartoon child floating above a crescent moon in space with a big question mark, then a second image of the same child jumping for joy on the ground with a check mark.
practiceBody: Now you know the difference between literal and nonliteral language! Read each passage and figure out what the words and phrases really mean. You are going to crush it!
practiceImage: A cartoon student flexing their brain muscles while surrounded by floating phrases like "piece of cake" and "break a leg" with their real meanings shown below each one.

title: Parts of Stories, Dramas, and Poems
introBody: Did you know stories, plays, and poems are built in special parts? Stories have chapters, plays have scenes, and poems have stanzas. Today we will learn the names of these parts and how each one builds on the one before it!
introImage: A cartoon construction crane stacking colorful blocks labeled "Chapter 1," "Scene 2," and "Stanza 3" to build a tall tower shaped like a book.
teachBody: A chapter is a section of a book. A scene is a part of a play where the action happens in one place. A stanza is a group of lines in a poem, kind of like a paragraph. Each part adds something new and builds on what came before.
teachImage: Three side-by-side cartoon panels: a book open to "Chapter 3," a theater stage with curtains and a "Scene 2" sign, and a poem on paper with stanzas highlighted in different colors.
exampleBody: Imagine a story: Chapter 1 introduces a girl who finds a mysterious key. Chapter 2 shows her searching for the lock it opens. Chapter 3 reveals a hidden room! Each chapter builds on the last one, making the story more exciting.
exampleImage: Three connected cartoon panels showing a girl finding a golden key, then searching through a spooky hallway, then opening a glowing hidden door to a magical room.
tipHeading: Think About What Each Part Adds!
tipBody: After you read a chapter, scene, or stanza, pause and ask yourself what new information it gave you. How does it connect to what you already read? Each part is like a puzzle piece that fits into the bigger picture.
tipImage: A cartoon child placing a glowing puzzle piece into a large jigsaw puzzle that shows a complete story scene forming as pieces connect.
practiceBody: Now you know about chapters, scenes, and stanzas! Read each passage and think about how the different parts connect and build on each other. You are a structure superstar!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a hard hat and holding blueprints, standing next to a completed building made of books, with a banner that reads "Master Builder of Stories."

title: Your Point of View vs. the Character's
introHeading: Seeing the Story Through Different Eyes!
introBody: When you read a story, you might think differently than the characters or narrator do. That is totally okay! Today we will learn how to tell the difference between your own point of view and the point of view of the people in the story.
introImage: Two cartoon children looking at the same painting but seeing different things: one sees a dragon, and the other sees a friendly dinosaur, with thought bubbles above each.
teachBody: Point of view is how someone sees and thinks about things. The narrator or characters in a story have their own opinions and feelings. You have yours too! Sometimes you might agree with a character, and sometimes you might think they are making a mistake.
teachImage: A cartoon narrator sitting in an armchair telling a story, with a thought bubble showing their version, while a child reader nearby has a different thought bubble showing their own idea.
exampleBody: Read this: Tom thought it was a great idea to skip practice and play video games. You might think Tom is making a bad choice because practice is important. Tom's point of view is that games are more fun. Your point of view might be that practice helps you get better!
exampleImage: A split-screen cartoon: on one side, a boy happily playing video games with a thumbs up. On the other side, a reader shaking their head while imagining the boy missing an important game.
tipHeading: Ask: Do I Agree or Disagree?
tipBody: As you read, ask yourself if you agree with what the character is doing or thinking. If you disagree, that shows your point of view is different from theirs. Both views are valid, but it is important to notice the difference!
tipImage: A cartoon child holding two signs: one green sign saying "I agree!" and one red sign saying "I disagree!" while reading an open book on a table.
practiceBody: Now you understand that readers and characters can see things differently! Read each story and think about how your feelings compare to the characters' feelings. You have got this!
practiceImage: A cartoon student looking through a pair of special binoculars labeled "My View" on one lens and "Character's View" on the other, with a big smile.

title: How Pictures Help Tell the Story
introBody: The illustrations in a book are not just for decoration. They actually help tell the story! Today we will learn how pictures work together with words to help you understand what is happening. Get your eyes ready!
introImage: A cartoon book lying open with characters literally stepping out of the illustrations and into the real world, blending pictures with floating text.
teachBody: Illustrations can show you things the words do not say. They can show a character's feelings through facial expressions, reveal details about the setting, or show action that makes the story come alive. Words and pictures are a team!
teachImage: A cartoon page from a book where the text says "She was nervous" and the illustration shows a girl biting her nails with wide eyes, adding detail the words alone did not give.
exampleBody: Read this: The knight entered the dragon's cave. If the illustration shows the cave is dark and filled with treasure, we learn extra details! The words told us he went in. The picture shows us what the cave looks like. Together, they tell the full story.
exampleImage: A cartoon knight with a torch stepping into a dark cave filled with gold coins and jewels, with a sleeping dragon in the background curled around a treasure pile.
tipBody: Do not skip the illustrations! Look at the characters' faces to see how they feel. Check the background for clues about where and when the story takes place. Illustrations often show details that the words leave out.
tipImage: A cartoon magnifying glass hovering over a book illustration, with arrows pointing to a character's smile, a clock on the wall, and rain outside a window.
practiceBody: You now know that illustrations are just as important as words! Look at the pictures and text together to understand each story fully. Time to put your skills to work!
practiceImage: A cartoon student holding up a finished picture frame containing a scene from a story, with words and images perfectly blended together, and a ribbon that says "Picture Expert."

title: Comparing Stories by the Same Author
introBody: Have you ever read two books by the same author? Sometimes authors write stories with the same characters but in different settings or with different problems. Today we will learn how to compare and contrast themes, settings, and plots across these stories!
introImage: Two cartoon book covers side by side by the same author: one shows a mouse on a sailboat, the other shows the same mouse in a snowy mountain, with a "VS" symbol between them.
teachBody: When you compare stories, you look for what is the same. When you contrast, you look for what is different. Two stories by the same author might share similar themes like friendship but have different settings and plots. A Venn diagram can help you organize your thinking!
teachImage: A large cartoon Venn diagram on a whiteboard with "Story 1" on the left, "Story 2" on the right, and "Both" in the middle, with a cartoon teacher pointing at it.
exampleBody: Imagine an author writes two stories about a dog named Max. In Story 1, Max helps a lost kitten find its way home at the park. In Story 2, Max helps a baby bird back to its nest at the beach. Both share the theme of helping others, but the settings and plots are different!
exampleImage: A cartoon dog helping a kitten in a green park on the left, and the same dog helping a baby bird at a sandy beach on the right, connected by a banner reading "Same hero, different adventure!"
tipBody: Draw two overlapping circles. In the left circle, write what is unique to Story 1. In the right circle, write what is unique to Story 2. In the middle, write what they share. This makes comparing and contrasting super easy!
tipImage: A cartoon child happily drawing a colorful Venn diagram on a big sheet of paper with crayons, filling in details about two stories.
practiceBody: Now you can spot what is the same and different between stories by the same author! Read each pair of stories and compare their themes, settings, and plots. Let's do this!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing judge robes holding two books and a scorecard, ready to compare them, with a gavel and a banner that reads "Story Comparison Expert."

title: Reading Challenging Stories
introBody: You have been learning so many reading skills this year! Now it is time to put them all together and tackle some tougher stories. Today we will practice reading and understanding stories that challenge us. You are ready for this!
introImage: A cartoon student climbing a mountain made of stacked books, almost reaching the peak where a golden flag reads "3rd Grade Reading Champion!"
teachBody: When you read a challenging story, use every tool you have learned. Ask questions, find the main message, notice how characters' actions drive the plot, and pay attention to tricky language. Combining all these skills makes you a powerful reader!
teachImage: A cartoon toolbox overflowing with colorful tools, each labeled with a reading skill: "Questions," "Main Idea," "Characters," "Vocabulary," and "Text Evidence."
exampleBody: Read this: Though the old lighthouse keeper grumbled about the endless storms, he never missed a night tending the light, for the sailors depended on him. This text uses bigger words, but we can figure it out! The keeper complains but still does his job because others need him.
exampleImage: An old cartoon lighthouse keeper in a yellow raincoat climbing the spiral stairs of a tall lighthouse during a wild thunderstorm, with a ship visible in the distance.
tipBody: Challenging stories sometimes need to be read more than once. That is totally normal! Slow down, reread tricky parts, and use everything you have learned this year. Every time you reread, you will understand more.
tipImage: A cartoon child reading a book slowly with a turtle beside them, and a speech bubble saying "Take your time!" with a rewind arrow symbol above the book.
practiceBody: You have all the reading skills you need to handle challenging stories! Take your time, use your strategies, and show what you know. You are a reading superstar!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing on top of a mountain of books wearing a cape that says "Super Reader," with fireworks and confetti in the sky behind them.

title: Finding Answers in Nonfiction
introBody: Nonfiction texts are full of amazing facts and information. Today we will learn how to ask and answer questions about nonfiction by going back to the text and finding the evidence. Let's become fact-finding experts!
introImage: A cartoon student dressed as a newspaper reporter with a notepad and pen, standing in front of shelves packed with nonfiction books about animals, space, and history.
teachBody: When you answer a question about a nonfiction text, you need to point to the exact part that supports your answer. This is called referring to the text. Do not just guess. Go back, find the sentence, and use it as proof!
teachImage: A cartoon magnifying glass zooming in on a specific sentence in a nonfiction article about volcanoes, with an arrow connecting the sentence to a question card.
exampleBody: Read this: Dolphins are mammals that live in the ocean. They breathe air through a blowhole on top of their head. Question: How do dolphins breathe? Answer: Through a blowhole on top of their head! The second sentence gave us the evidence we needed.
exampleImage: A cartoon dolphin leaping out of the ocean and breathing through its blowhole, with a labeled arrow pointing to the blowhole and a speech bubble saying "That's how I breathe!"
tipBody: Read the question first, then look for key words from the question in the text. When you find a match, you are close to the answer! Underlining or highlighting helps you keep track of important evidence.
tipImage: A cartoon hand with a yellow highlighter underlining key words in a nonfiction paragraph about penguins, with matching key words circled in the question above.
practiceBody: You are now a fact-finding expert! Read each nonfiction passage and use evidence from the text to answer the questions. Show that you can refer to the text like a pro!
practiceImage: A cartoon student holding a magnifying glass over an open nonfiction book with sticky notes and highlights everywhere, wearing a badge that says "Fact Finder."

title: Main Idea and Key Details
introBody: Every nonfiction text has a main idea, which is the most important point the author wants you to know. Today we will learn how to find the main idea and figure out which details support it. Let's get started!
introImage: A cartoon umbrella labeled "Main Idea" sheltering several smaller items labeled "Detail 1," "Detail 2," and "Detail 3" from the rain.
teachHeading: The Main Idea Is the Big Point
teachBody: The main idea is what the whole text is mostly about. Key details are the facts and examples that support the main idea. Think of the main idea as a table and the details as the legs holding it up. Without the details, the table falls down!
teachImage: A cartoon table labeled "Main Idea" standing firmly on four legs, each labeled with a key detail like "fact," "example," "reason," and "evidence."
exampleBody: Read this: Bees are very important to our world. They help flowers grow by spreading pollen. They also make honey that people and animals eat. Without bees, many plants could not survive. The main idea is that bees are important. The details explain why!
exampleImage: A cartoon bee flying between colorful flowers and spreading golden pollen, with a jar of honey nearby and a globe in the background with a heart on it.
tipHeading: Ask: What Is This Mostly About?
tipBody: After reading, ask yourself what the whole passage is mostly about in one sentence. That is your main idea! Then check that the details in the text support that idea. If a detail does not fit, the main idea might need adjusting.
tipImage: A cartoon child with a thinking cap on, surrounded by floating detail cards, placing them under a banner that reads "Main Idea" to see which ones fit.
practiceBody: You are ready to find main ideas and key details like a pro! Read each passage and figure out the big point the author is making. Then find the details that support it. Go for it!
practiceImage: A cartoon student assembling a puzzle where the center piece says "Main Idea" and the surrounding pieces say "Key Detail," with the last piece about to snap into place.

title: Connections in Time and Cause and Effect
introBody: Nonfiction texts often describe events in order or explain why things happen. Today we will learn to describe relationships between events using time, sequence, and cause and effect. Let's connect the dots!
introImage: A cartoon timeline stretching across a wall showing historical events connected by arrows, with a curious student pointing at the connections between them.
teachBody: Authors use words like "first," "next," "then," and "finally" to show the order of events. They use words like "because," "so," and "as a result" to show cause and effect. These signal words help you understand how events are connected!
teachImage: A cartoon signpost at a crossroads with arrows pointing in different directions, labeled "First," "Next," "Because," "As a result," and "Finally."
exampleBody: Read this: First, the caterpillar eats lots of leaves. Next, it forms a chrysalis. Finally, it becomes a butterfly! The words "first," "next," and "finally" show us the order. The caterpillar eating causes it to grow, which leads to its transformation.
exampleImage: A cartoon sequence showing a green caterpillar munching leaves, then hanging in a chrysalis from a branch, then emerging as a beautiful orange and blue butterfly.
tipBody: Circle or underline signal words like "because," "first," "then," "after," and "as a result" when you read. These words are like road signs that show you how events connect to each other.
tipImage: A cartoon child driving a small car along a road lined with signs that say "Because," "Then," "So," and "Finally," leading to a finish line labeled "I understand!"
practiceBody: Now you know how to spot time order and cause and effect in nonfiction texts! Read each passage and find the signal words that show how events connect. You are going to do great!
practiceImage: A cartoon student connecting dots on a large board with string, like a detective, with labels showing events linked by cause and effect arrows.

title: Academic and Domain-Specific Words
introBody: Nonfiction texts often use special vocabulary words that are important for understanding the topic. Today we will learn how to figure out the meaning of academic and domain-specific words and phrases. Let's power up our vocabulary!
introImage: A cartoon student flexing their arms, with word bubbles floating around them containing words like "habitat," "equation," "democracy," and "photosynthesis."
teachBody: Domain-specific words are special words used in a particular subject. For example, "habitat" is a science word and "continent" is a geography word. Academic words like "compare" and "analyze" are used across all subjects. Context clues in the sentence can help you figure out what these words mean!
teachImage: A cartoon bookshelf with sections labeled "Science Words," "Math Words," "History Words," and "General Academic Words," each shelf holding word cards.
exampleBody: Read this: The desert habitat is very dry, with sandy soil and very little rain. The word "habitat" might be new to you, but the sentence tells us it is a place where things live. The clues "dry," "sandy soil," and "little rain" describe this habitat!
exampleImage: A cartoon desert scene with cacti, sand dunes, and a lizard, with the word "habitat" glowing above the scene and arrows pointing to context clues in the sand.
tipBody: When you find a tricky word, read the words and sentences around it. Often the author gives you clues about what the word means. Definitions, examples, and descriptions nearby can help you unlock the meaning.
tipImage: A cartoon flashlight shining on a difficult word in the center of a paragraph, illuminating the surrounding context clues that glow in different colors.
practiceBody: You are now equipped to tackle academic and domain-specific words! Read each passage and use context clues to figure out what the special vocabulary means. You have got the power!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a cape and holding a dictionary like a shield, standing heroically on a pile of vocabulary flashcards with a banner saying "Word Champion."

title: Using Text Features to Find Information
introBody: Nonfiction texts have special features that help you find information quickly. Today we will learn how to use text features and search tools like keywords, sidebars, and hyperlinks to locate what you need. Think of them as a GPS for reading!
introImage: A cartoon student holding a tablet with a nonfiction article on screen, with glowing arrows pointing to the table of contents, sidebar, bold keywords, and hyperlinks.
teachBody: Text features include headings, bold words, sidebars, tables of contents, indexes, and hyperlinks. Headings tell you what a section is about. Bold words signal important vocabulary. Sidebars give extra facts. These features help you find information without reading every single word!
teachImage: A cartoon nonfiction page with labeled arrows pointing to a heading, a bold keyword, a sidebar with fun facts, a photograph with a caption, and a hyperlink in blue.
exampleBody: Imagine you need to find out how long sea turtles live. You could scan the headings for one called "Life Span" or look in the index for "life span." Then you go right to that section! You do not have to read the whole book to find your answer.
exampleImage: A cartoon child flipping through a book about sea turtles, with a finger pointing at a heading that reads "Life Span" and a highlighted sentence saying "Sea turtles can live over 80 years."
tipBody: Before diving into a nonfiction text, scan the headings, bold words, and pictures first. This gives you a quick preview of what the text is about and helps you know where to look when you need specific information.
tipImage: A cartoon child's eyes scanning across a nonfiction page, with dotted lines showing their gaze jumping from heading to heading, bold word to bold word, like a search pattern.
practiceBody: You now know how to use text features like a navigation expert! Read each passage and use the features to find the information you need quickly. Happy searching!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing at a control panel full of buttons labeled "Heading," "Bold Word," "Sidebar," and "Index," confidently pressing the right button to find an answer.

title: Your Point of View vs. the Author's
introBody: When you read a nonfiction text, the author has opinions and so do you! Today we will learn how to tell the difference between your own point of view and the author's point of view. This makes you a smarter, more thoughtful reader!
introImage: A cartoon author writing at a desk on the left and a cartoon student reading at a desk on the right, each with a thought bubble showing a different opinion about the same topic.
teachBody: Authors sometimes share their opinions in nonfiction writing. They might think recycling is the most important thing people can do, or that dogs make better pets than cats. You might agree or disagree, and that is okay! The key is to notice when the author is sharing an opinion.
teachImage: A cartoon book page with highlighted sentences: one in blue labeled "Author's opinion" and another in green labeled "Fact," with a cartoon student circling the opinion sentence.
exampleBody: Read this: Everyone should eat vegetables because they are the best food in the world. The author thinks vegetables are the best food. You might think pizza is better! The author's point of view and yours are different, and you can explain why you agree or disagree.
exampleImage: A cartoon split screen: on one side, an author happily eating a plate of colorful vegetables. On the other side, a student holding a slice of pizza with a thought bubble saying "I disagree!"
tipBody: Words like "should," "best," "most important," and "everyone must" are clues that the author is sharing an opinion. When you spot these words, pause and ask yourself if you agree or disagree. That is your point of view!
tipImage: A cartoon spotlight shining on opinion words in a paragraph: "should," "best," and "must" are glowing and circled in red, with a student nodding thoughtfully.
practiceBody: You can now tell the difference between your point of view and the author's! Read each passage, find the author's opinion, and decide how you feel about it. You are a critical thinker!
practiceImage: A cartoon student sitting in a judge's chair with a gavel, weighing two sides on a balance scale: one side says "Author's View" and the other says "My View."

title: Learning from Maps, Photos, and Text
introBody: In nonfiction texts, illustrations like maps, photographs, and diagrams give you important information. Today we will learn how to combine what the pictures show with what the words say to understand the text even better!
introImage: A cartoon student looking at a nonfiction book spread showing a colorful map of the ocean on one page and a photograph of a whale on the other, with fact labels floating above.
teachBody: Maps show where things are located. Photographs show what things really look like. Diagrams explain how things work. When you combine the information from these illustrations with the words in the text, you get a much fuller picture of the topic!
teachImage: Three cartoon panels: a treasure map with a route marked, a photograph of a coral reef, and a labeled diagram of a plant, each adding information that words alone could not show.
exampleHeading: Read the Map and the Text!
exampleBody: Read this: The Sahara Desert is in northern Africa. It is the largest hot desert in the world. If a map shows the Sahara colored yellow, you can see exactly where it is and how big it is. The words tell you it is the largest, and the map shows you its size!
exampleImage: A cartoon map of Africa with the Sahara Desert highlighted in golden yellow, next to a text box saying "Largest hot desert in the world" with a cartoon camel standing on the map.
tipBody: Do not skip maps, photos, or diagrams when you read nonfiction. Look at the labels, captions, and legends. They give you extra information that the text might not include. Together, the words and pictures make you an expert!
tipImage: A cartoon child with a magnifying glass examining a diagram of the water cycle, reading the labels and captions with arrows pointing from the text to the diagram.
practiceBody: You now know how to use illustrations and text together to learn more! Look at the maps, photos, and diagrams in each passage to help you answer the questions. You are an information superstar!
practiceImage: A cartoon student pinning photographs, maps, and text cards onto a large corkboard, connecting them with colored string to show how they work together.

title: How Sentences and Paragraphs Connect
introBody: In nonfiction writing, sentences and paragraphs are connected in logical ways. Today we will learn how authors link ideas using comparison, cause and effect, and sequence. Understanding these connections makes you a stronger reader!
introImage: A cartoon chain made of colorful links, each link labeled with a sentence or idea, stretching across the page and connecting to form a complete paragraph.
teachBody: Authors connect sentences and paragraphs in different ways. Comparison shows how two things are alike or different. Cause and effect shows why something happened. Sequence shows the order things happen, like first, second, and third. Spotting these patterns helps you understand the text!
teachImage: Three cartoon road signs: one showing two items being compared with a scale, one showing a lightning bolt causing rain, and one showing numbered steps 1-2-3.
exampleBody: Read this: Cats are independent pets. Dogs, on the other hand, need more attention and walks. This is a comparison! The author is showing how cats and dogs are different. The phrase "on the other hand" is the clue that the author is contrasting the two animals.
exampleImage: A cartoon cat lounging independently on a couch on one side, and a cartoon dog eagerly waiting by the door with a leash in its mouth on the other side, with a "VS" symbol between them.
tipBody: Authors use special words to connect ideas. "However" and "but" signal contrast. "Because" and "so" signal cause and effect. "First" and "then" signal sequence. Highlight these words to see how the ideas are linked!
tipImage: A cartoon highlighter circling linking words in a paragraph: "however" in pink, "because" in blue, and "first" in green, with lines connecting them to their matching ideas.
practiceBody: You can now spot how sentences and paragraphs connect! Read each passage and look for comparisons, cause and effect, and sequence. You are a connection detective!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a detective hat and holding a clipboard, with a web of connected ideas on a whiteboard behind them, all linked by arrows and labels.

title: Comparing Two Texts on the Same Topic
introBody: Sometimes you will read two different texts about the same topic. Each text might share different facts or focus on different details. Today we will learn how to compare and contrast the most important points from two texts. Let's become comparison pros!
introImage: Two cartoon nonfiction books about space sitting on a desk: one titled "The Sun" and the other "Stars in the Sky," with overlapping fact bubbles rising from both books.
teachHeading: Find What's the Same and Different
teachBody: When you read two texts on the same topic, look for the most important points in each one. Some points will be the same in both texts, and some will be different. Comparing helps you get a more complete understanding of the topic!
teachImage: A cartoon Venn diagram with "Text 1" on the left, "Text 2" on the right, and "Both" in the middle, with fact cards being sorted into each section by a cartoon student.
exampleBody: Text 1 says recycling saves trees and reduces waste. Text 2 says recycling saves energy and creates jobs. Both texts agree that recycling is good for the planet! But each one highlights different benefits. Together, they give us a bigger picture.
exampleImage: Two cartoon newspaper clippings about recycling side by side: one shows a tree being saved, the other shows a factory powered by recycled materials, with a green Earth smiling in the center.
tipBody: Draw a T-chart with "Text 1" on one side and "Text 2" on the other. Write down the most important points from each text. Then look for points that match and points that are unique. This makes comparing super organized!
tipImage: A cartoon child drawing a neat T-chart on a large poster board, writing key points from two texts in each column with colored markers.
practiceBody: You are ready to compare two texts like an expert! Read each pair of passages and find what is the same and what is different. Use your comparison skills to understand each topic more fully!
practiceImage: A cartoon student holding two magnifying glasses, one over each of two open books, with sparkles showing the similarities and differences they have discovered.

title: Reading Challenging Nonfiction
introBody: You have been building up your nonfiction reading muscles all year! Now it is time to tackle some challenging informational texts. Today we will use all our skills together to understand tougher passages. Let's show what we can do!
introImage: A cartoon student in a superhero outfit lifting a giant nonfiction book overhead like a weight, with beams of light radiating from the book.
teachBody: When a nonfiction text feels hard, do not give up! Use all the strategies you have learned: find the main idea, look for text features, use context clues for tricky words, and check the illustrations. Putting these skills together helps you understand any text!
teachImage: A cartoon shield divided into four quadrants, each showing a strategy: a magnifying glass for "Main Idea," a map for "Text Features," a lightbulb for "Context Clues," and a picture frame for "Illustrations."
exampleBody: Read this: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert sunlight into food. The leaves absorb light and combine it with water and carbon dioxide. That is a big word, but the text tells us what it means! Plants turn sunlight into food using their leaves.
exampleImage: A cartoon plant with smiling leaves absorbing golden sunlight rays, with arrows showing water going up from the roots and food being made inside the leaf.
tipBody: If a passage seems overwhelming, break it into smaller chunks. Read one paragraph at a time and summarize it in your own words before moving on. Small steps add up to big understanding!
tipImage: A cartoon student cutting a long nonfiction passage into sections with scissors, then reading each small section one at a time with a satisfied smile.
practiceBody: You have all the tools to conquer challenging nonfiction texts! Take your time, use your strategies, and show off everything you have learned this year. You are amazing!
practiceImage: A cartoon student crossing a finish line tape that reads "Nonfiction Champion!" while holding an open informational text above their head in triumph.

title: Phonics and Word Analysis
introBody: Phonics is like a code that helps you read new words. Today we will learn how to use phonics and word analysis skills to decode tricky words. Once you crack the code, you can read almost anything!
introImage: A cartoon student wearing spy sunglasses and holding a magnifying glass over a page of words, with letters glowing and rearranging into readable words.
teachHeading: Breaking Words Apart to Read Them
teachBody: When you see a word you do not know, break it into smaller parts. Look for prefixes at the beginning, root words in the middle, and suffixes at the end. Sound out each part, then blend them together. This is how great readers decode new words!
teachImage: A cartoon word "unhappy" being split into three colorful puzzle pieces: "un" in red, "happy" in blue, showing how the word breaks apart and comes back together.
exampleBody: Let's decode the word "rewriting." Break it apart: "re" means again, "writ" is the root from "write," and "ing" means it is happening now. Put it together: rewriting means writing again! You just decoded a big word by using its parts.
exampleImage: A cartoon conveyor belt in a word factory, with the word "rewriting" being assembled piece by piece: "re" drops in, then "write," then "ing," and the complete word rolls out shining.
tipHeading: Sound It Out, Part by Part!
tipBody: If a word looks hard, cover up parts of it and sound out one piece at a time. Start with the beginning, then the middle, then the end. Blend the sounds together and you will have the whole word!
tipImage: A cartoon child covering parts of a long word on a whiteboard with sticky notes, revealing one syllable at a time and sounding each one out with speech bubbles.
practiceBody: You are ready to decode words using phonics and word analysis! Break each word into parts, sound them out, and blend them together. You are a word-cracking superstar!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing in front of a cracked safe that has opened to reveal a stack of decoded words inside, with a banner reading "Code Cracker!"

title: Prefixes and Suffixes
introBody: Did you know that adding a small part to the beginning or end of a word can change its whole meaning? Today we will learn about the most common prefixes and suffixes and what they mean. This is a superpower for reading!
introImage: A cartoon magician waving a wand over the word "happy," and with a poof, the prefix "un" appears and the word transforms into "unhappy" with a different emoji face.
teachHeading: Prefixes Go at the Beginning, Suffixes at the End
teachBody: A prefix is added to the beginning of a word to change its meaning. "Un" means not, so "unkind" means not kind. A suffix is added to the end. "Ful" means full of, so "careful" means full of care. Knowing these parts helps you understand new words instantly!
teachImage: A cartoon word train with three cars: the first car labeled "Prefix" carries "un," the middle car labeled "Root" carries "break," and the last car labeled "Suffix" carries "able," forming "unbreakable."
exampleBody: Look at the word "disagree." The prefix "dis" means not or opposite. So "disagree" means to not agree. Now look at "playful." The suffix "ful" means full of. So "playful" means full of play! See how the small parts change the meaning?
exampleImage: Two cartoon scenes side by side: two children shaking their heads and disagreeing over a board game, and a playful puppy bouncing around with a ball, with the words labeled above each scene.
tipBody: Memorize a few key prefixes and suffixes: "un" means not, "re" means again, "pre" means before, "ful" means full of, "less" means without, and "ly" makes an adverb. These six will help you unlock hundreds of new words!
tipImage: A cartoon key ring with six colorful keys, each labeled with a prefix or suffix: "un," "re," "pre," "ful," "less," and "ly," with a treasure chest of words waiting to be unlocked.
practiceBody: You now know how prefixes and suffixes work! Use your knowledge of these word parts to figure out the meaning of new words. You are a word wizard!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a wizard hat and robe, casting a spell that splits words into their prefix, root, and suffix parts, with sparkles flying everywhere.

title: Words with Latin Suffixes
introHeading: Ancient Word Parts Still Help Us Today!
introBody: Many English words have endings that come from the Latin language, which was spoken thousands of years ago. Today we will learn to decode words with common Latin suffixes. This will help you read and understand lots of big words!
introImage: A cartoon Roman column with Latin suffixes carved into it like "tion," "sion," "ment," and "able," with a modern-day student reading them with amazement.
teachHeading: Common Latin Suffixes You Should Know
teachBody: Latin suffixes like "tion" and "sion" turn verbs into nouns. "Educate" becomes "education." The suffix "ment" also makes nouns: "enjoy" becomes "enjoyment." The suffix "able" means can be done: "read" becomes "readable." Recognizing these suffixes helps you decode big words!
teachImage: A cartoon chart on a classroom wall showing Latin suffixes in one column and example words in another: "tion = action," "sion = mission," "ment = movement," "able = comfortable."
exampleBody: Let's try the word "celebration." The root is "celebrate" and the Latin suffix is "tion," which turns it into a noun meaning the act of celebrating. Or take "comfortable." The root is "comfort" and the suffix "able" means it can be done. Comfortable means able to give comfort!
exampleImage: A cartoon party scene labeled "celebration" with the word split into "celebrate" + "tion" above a banner, and confetti and balloons everywhere.
tipBody: When you see a long word, look at the end first. If you spot a Latin suffix like "tion," "sion," "ment," or "able," cover it up and read the root word. Then add the suffix meaning. You just decoded a big word!
tipImage: A cartoon child using their hand to cover the suffix "tion" on the word "invention" written on a chalkboard, reading "invent" first, then uncovering "tion" to read the full word.
practiceBody: You can now decode words with Latin suffixes! Look for these endings in each word and use them to figure out the meaning. You are becoming a word history expert!
practiceImage: A cartoon student dressed as a Roman scholar with a laurel wreath, holding a scroll that unrolls to reveal decoded words with their Latin suffixes highlighted.

title: Decoding Multisyllable Words
introHeading: Big Words Are Just Small Parts Together!
introBody: Some words look really long and scary, but they are just made of smaller parts called syllables. Today we will learn how to break big words into syllables so you can read them easily. No word is too big for you!
introImage: A cartoon student karate-chopping a long word into syllable pieces that fly apart and then come back together, with confidence stars around them.
teachHeading: How to Break Words into Syllables
teachBody: Every syllable has one vowel sound. Clap the beats in a word to count the syllables. "Butterfly" has three claps: but-ter-fly. You can also look for patterns like two consonants together in the middle, which is often where you split the word.
teachImage: A cartoon word "watermelon" being split by dotted lines into four syllables: wa-ter-mel-on, with a cartoon hand clapping four times below.
exampleBody: Try the word "basketball." Clap it out: bas-ket-ball. Three syllables! Each part is easy to read on its own. Now blend them together: basketball! See? Even a word with ten letters is easy when you break it into smaller parts.
exampleImage: A cartoon basketball bouncing three times, with each bounce labeled "bas," "ket," and "ball," and the full word "basketball" appearing when it lands.
tipBody: Here are three steps for any big word: First, clap the syllables. Second, split the word where you hear the breaks. Third, blend the parts back together. Clap, split, blend. That is your secret formula!
tipImage: A cartoon poster showing three steps with icons: Step 1 has clapping hands, Step 2 has scissors cutting a word, Step 3 has glue putting the word back together.
practiceBody: You are a syllable-splitting champion! Use the clap, split, blend strategy to decode every big word you see. No word is too long for you now!
practiceImage: A cartoon student flexing their muscles next to a scoreboard showing increasingly long words they have successfully decoded, with a trophy at the top.

title: Tricky Irregularly Spelled Words
introBody: Most words follow phonics rules, but some words are rebels! They are spelled in tricky ways that do not follow the usual patterns. Today we will practice reading these irregularly spelled words so they do not trip you up anymore!
introImage: A lineup of cartoon words with attitude: "enough," "through," and "island" wearing sunglasses and leather jackets, with a sign that says "We break the rules!"
teachHeading: You Just Have to Know Them
teachBody: Irregularly spelled words like "said," "friend," "through," and "enough" do not sound the way they look. You cannot always sound these words out using normal phonics rules. The best way to learn them is to practice reading them over and over until they become familiar!
teachImage: A cartoon flashcard wall with tricky words like "could," "people," "enough," "friend," "island," and "beautiful," each on a colorful card with a star sticker.
exampleBody: Look at the word "laugh." The "gh" makes an "f" sound, which is unusual! And "island" has a silent "s" in it. These words are tricky, but once you have read them a few times, your brain remembers them. You already know more tricky words than you think!
exampleImage: A cartoon brain with a filing cabinet inside, pulling out a card labeled "laugh" and filing it under "Words I Know," while a new card labeled "island" waits to be filed.
tipHeading: Read, Read, and Read Some More!
tipBody: The more you read, the more you will see these tricky words. Each time you read one, your brain gets better at recognizing it instantly. Make flashcards of tricky words and practice them to build your word power!
tipImage: A cartoon child sitting in a cozy reading nook, flipping through a book with tricky words highlighted, and a growing stack of "Words I Mastered" flashcards beside them.
practiceBody: You are ready to tackle those tricky, irregularly spelled words! Read each one carefully and remember that practice makes perfect. You have got this!
practiceImage: A cartoon student lassoing tricky words like a cowboy, pulling "enough," "through," and "island" into a corral labeled "Words I Can Read!"

title: Reading with Accuracy and Fluency
introBody: Great readers do not just read words correctly. They also read smoothly and understand what they are reading. Today we will learn about accuracy and fluency, two skills that help you become a confident reader. Let's get flowing!
introImage: A cartoon student reading a book with smooth sound waves flowing from the pages, like a river of words, with musical notes and a serene expression on their face.
teachBody: Accuracy means reading words correctly. Fluency means reading at a good speed, with expression, and not too choppy. When you are both accurate and fluent, your brain can focus on understanding the meaning instead of struggling with each word. It all works together!
teachImage: A cartoon equation on a chalkboard: a checkmark labeled "Accuracy" plus a flowing river labeled "Fluency" equals a lightbulb labeled "Comprehension," with a teacher pointing at it.
exampleBody: Listen to two ways of reading the same sentence. Choppy: "The... big... dog... ran... fast." Fluent: "The big dog ran fast!" The second way sounds natural and helps you picture the dog running. Fluent reading brings the story to life!
exampleImage: Two cartoon speech bubbles: one with broken, choppy text and a confused face, and one with smooth flowing text and a happy face, with a cartoon dog running in the background.
tipBody: The best way to build fluency is to read aloud every day. Start with books that are comfortable for you, then gradually try harder ones. Reread favorite passages to get smoother each time. Practice makes your reading flow like a river!
tipImage: A cartoon child reading aloud from a book to a group of stuffed animals arranged in an audience, with smooth flowing words coming from the child's mouth.
practiceBody: You are on your way to reading with accuracy and fluency! Read each passage carefully, paying attention to both the words and the flow. You are going to sound amazing!
practiceImage: A cartoon student on a stage reading into a microphone with confidence, with an audience of classmates giving a standing ovation and a banner that says "Fluency Star!"

title: Reading with Purpose and Understanding
introBody: Every time you read, you should have a purpose. Maybe you are reading to learn something new, to enjoy a story, or to find a specific fact. Today we will learn how reading with a purpose helps you understand what you read so much better!
introImage: A cartoon student holding a compass labeled "Purpose" while walking through a library, with arrows on the ground pointing toward different sections like "Learn," "Enjoy," and "Discover."
teachHeading: Set a Purpose Before You Read
teachBody: Before you start reading, ask yourself why you are reading this text. Are you looking for information? Enjoying a story? Studying for a test? When you know your purpose, your brain focuses on the right things and you understand more!
teachImage: A cartoon student at a desk with three thought bubbles: one shows a magnifying glass for "finding facts," one shows a heart for "enjoying a story," and one shows a lightbulb for "learning something new."
exampleBody: Imagine reading a recipe. Your purpose is to learn how to bake cookies. So you pay close attention to the steps and ingredients. If you were reading the same text just for fun, you might skip some details. Your purpose guides what you focus on!
exampleImage: A cartoon child in a kitchen reading a recipe book very carefully, with ingredients and measuring cups laid out on the counter, focused and ready to bake chocolate chip cookies.
tipHeading: Ask: Why Am I Reading This?
tipBody: Before opening any book or article, take five seconds to ask yourself why you are reading it. Then as you read, check in with yourself. Am I getting what I need? This simple habit will boost your understanding every single time!
tipImage: A cartoon sticky note on a book that says "My Purpose:" with a blank line, and a cartoon pencil writing "To learn about volcanoes" on the line.
practiceBody: You now know how to read with purpose! Before each passage, think about why you are reading it. Then focus on understanding the text. Your purpose will guide you to success!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing at the top of a mountain with a flag that says "I Read with Purpose!" looking out over a landscape made of open books.

title: Reading Aloud with Expression
introBody: When you read aloud, your voice can make the words come alive! Today we will learn how to read prose and poetry with accuracy, at the right speed, and with expression. Get ready to become a reading performer!
introImage: A cartoon student standing on a small stage reading from a book of poetry, with colorful sound waves and musical notes coming from their mouth, and a small audience of friends listening.
teachBody: Accuracy means reading the words correctly. Rate means reading at the right speed, not too fast and not too slow. Expression means changing your voice to match the feeling. Make your voice excited for exciting parts, quiet for sad parts, and dramatic for surprises!
teachImage: A cartoon speedometer with three zones: "Too Slow" on the left, "Just Right" in the green middle, and "Too Fast" on the right, with a needle pointing to "Just Right."
exampleBody: Read this with expression: "Wait!" cried the princess. "The dragon is actually friendly!" Did you make your voice surprised for "Wait!" and amazed for the second sentence? That is expression! Your voice should match what the characters are feeling.
exampleImage: A cartoon princess with wide eyes and an outstretched hand, standing in front of a friendly smiling dragon, with a dramatic speech bubble above her head.
tipBody: Punctuation marks are like stage directions for your voice. A period means to pause. A question mark means your voice goes up. An exclamation point means to sound excited or loud. Commas mean a short pause. Let the punctuation guide your expression!
tipImage: A cartoon trail of punctuation marks on a path: a period is a stop sign, a question mark curves upward, an exclamation point has fireworks, and a comma is a speed bump.
practiceBody: You are ready to read aloud like a star performer! Pay attention to accuracy, speed, and expression as you read each passage. Make the words come alive with your voice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student taking a bow on a stage after a reading performance, with roses being thrown and a spotlight shining down, and a sign that says "Reading Performer of the Year!"

title: Using Context to Self-Correct
introBody: Even the best readers sometimes read a word wrong or get confused. The cool thing is, great readers catch their mistakes and fix them! Today we will learn how to use context clues to check your reading and self-correct when something does not make sense.
introImage: A cartoon student reading a book, stopping with a puzzled look, then rereading with a smile as a lightbulb appears above their head, showing the self-correction process.
teachBody: As you read, your brain should always be asking: does this make sense? If you read a word and the sentence sounds wrong, stop and try again. Look at the other words around it for clues. The context, meaning the words nearby, can help you figure out the right word!
teachImage: A cartoon brain with a red warning light flashing and a thought bubble saying "Wait, that does not make sense!" while looking at a sentence with one word circled in red.
exampleBody: Imagine you read: "The boy threw the ball to his frog." That sounds weird! You look again and realize the word is "friend," not "frog." The context, playing catch, helped you realize it should be "friend." You just self-corrected!
exampleImage: A cartoon boy about to throw a ball, with the word "frog" crossed out and replaced by "friend," and a happy friend with a baseball glove ready to catch.
tipBody: When something does not sound right, stop reading. Go back to the confusing word. Look at the letters again. Think about what word would make sense in the sentence. Then reread the whole sentence to make sure it flows. You are your own best editor!
tipImage: A cartoon three-step process poster: Step 1 shows a stop sign, Step 2 shows a rewind arrow over a word, Step 3 shows a checkmark with a smiling sentence.
practiceBody: You now know how to catch and fix your own reading mistakes! Read each passage carefully and use context to make sure everything makes sense. You are a self-correcting superstar!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a mechanic's outfit and holding a wrench, "fixing" words in a sentence on a workshop table, with a toolbox labeled "Context Clues."

title: Figuring Out Unknown Words
introBody: When you come across a word you do not know, do not panic! There are several strategies you can use to figure out its meaning. Today we will learn how to determine or clarify the meaning of unknown and multiple-meaning words. Let's get unlocking!
introImage: A cartoon student standing in front of a giant locked treasure chest labeled "Unknown Words," holding a ring of keys labeled "Context," "Word Parts," "Dictionary," and "Root Words."
teachBody: You have a whole toolkit for figuring out unknown words! You can use context clues from the sentence, break the word into parts like prefixes and suffixes, think about root words you already know, or look it up in a dictionary. Some words even have more than one meaning, and you have to pick the right one!
teachImage: A cartoon Swiss Army knife with each tool labeled differently: one blade says "Context Clues," another says "Word Parts," another says "Root Words," and the last says "Dictionary."
exampleBody: Read this: The baseball player made it to first base. Now read this: The base of the mountain was covered in snow. The word "base" has different meanings! In the first sentence it means a station in baseball. In the second, it means the bottom. Context tells you which meaning fits!
exampleImage: A split cartoon: on the left, a baseball player sliding into first base on a diamond. On the right, a snowy mountain with an arrow pointing to its base at the bottom.
tipHeading: Try the Meaning in the Sentence!
tipBody: When a word has more than one meaning, try each meaning in the sentence and see which one makes sense. If "bat" could mean a flying animal or a baseball stick, the rest of the sentence will tell you which one the author means!
tipImage: A cartoon child holding two cards: one showing a flying bat and one showing a baseball bat, looking at a sentence to decide which meaning fits, with an arrow pointing to the correct one.
practiceBody: You have a full toolkit for figuring out unknown and multiple-meaning words! Use all your strategies to unlock the meaning of every tricky word. You are a word detective!
practiceImage: A cartoon student opening the treasure chest of unknown words, with golden light and word meanings spilling out, and a detective badge pinned to their shirt.

title: Context Clues in Sentences
introBody: When you find a word you do not know, the rest of the sentence is full of clues! Today we will learn how to use sentence-level context to figure out what a word or phrase means. The answer is hiding right there in the sentence!
introImage: A cartoon sentence stretched out like a treasure map, with clue words glowing and dotted lines leading from them to the unknown word in the middle.
teachBody: Context clues are the words and sentences around an unknown word that help you figure out its meaning. Authors often include definitions, examples, or descriptions near tricky words. Read the whole sentence and think about what would make sense in that spot!
teachImage: A cartoon unknown word sitting in the center of a sentence, with surrounding words shining like stars and sending beams of light toward the mystery word to reveal its meaning.
exampleBody: Read this: The arid desert had not seen rain in months. Everything was dry and cracked. What does "arid" mean? The clues "no rain," "dry," and "cracked" tell us that "arid" means very dry! The sentence gave us everything we needed.
exampleImage: A cartoon desert scene with cracked earth, a blazing sun, and a withered cactus, with the word "arid" in the sky and arrows pointing from "dry" and "no rain" to it.
tipHeading: Read Before and After the Word!
tipBody: When you hit an unknown word, do not just stare at it. Read the words before it and after it. Often the author explains the word right in the same sentence or the next one. The clues are always nearby!
tipImage: A cartoon child reading a book with a glowing circle around an unknown word, and arrows pointing left and right to the surrounding clue words highlighted in yellow.
practiceBody: You are now a context clue expert! Read each sentence carefully and use the surrounding words to figure out the meaning of tricky words. The clues are right there waiting for you!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a safari hat, following a trail of glowing context clues through a jungle of words, reaching a clearing where the meaning is revealed on a golden sign.

title: Adding Affixes to Known Words
introBody: An affix is a prefix or suffix that you attach to a word to make a brand new word with a brand new meaning. Today we will learn how adding an affix to a word you already know creates a whole new word. It is like word math!
introImage: A cartoon student at a workbench assembling words like building blocks, snapping a prefix onto the front and a suffix onto the back of a root word.
teachBody: If you know the word "kind" and you add the prefix "un," you get "unkind," which means not kind. If you add the suffix "ness," you get "kindness," which means the quality of being kind. You already know the base word, so the affix just adds a new layer of meaning!
teachImage: A cartoon math equation on a whiteboard: the word "kind" plus the suffix "ness" equals "kindness," with a drawing of a heart next to it.
exampleBody: Start with the word "help." Add "ful" and you get "helpful," meaning full of help. Add "less" and you get "helpless," meaning without help. Add "er" and you get "helper," a person who helps. One word, three different affixes, three different meanings!
exampleImage: A cartoon tree with the trunk labeled "help" and three branches growing different words: "helpful" with a smiley face, "helpless" with a sad face, and "helper" with a person icon.
tipHeading: Know the Affix, Know the Word!
tipBody: If you learn what common affixes mean, you can figure out new words on the spot. "Re" means again, "un" means not, "er" means a person who does something, and "ness" means a state of being. Combine these with any word you know and you have instant understanding!
tipImage: A cartoon recipe card showing: "Take 1 known word + 1 affix = 1 new word!" with cartoon ingredients being mixed in a bowl labeled "Word Builder."
practiceBody: You are a word-building master! Take each known word, add an affix, and figure out the new meaning. This skill will help you read and understand so many more words!
practiceImage: A cartoon student proudly displaying a wall of words they built, each one showing the root word plus the affix in a different color, with a ribbon that says "Word Builder Award."

title: Root Words as Clues
introBody: A root word is the main part of a word that carries its core meaning. If you know a root word, you can figure out other words that share the same root! Today we will learn how to use root words as clues to unlock the meanings of unfamiliar words.
introImage: A cartoon tree with a thick root labeled "port" (meaning carry), and branches growing words like "transport," "portable," "import," and "export."
teachBody: Words that share the same root are like a family. They all have related meanings. If you know that "graph" means write or draw, then "autograph" is writing your own name, "photograph" is drawing with light, and "biography" is writing about someone's life. One root unlocks many words!
teachImage: A cartoon family photo frame with the root "graph" in the center, surrounded by family member words: "autograph," "paragraph," "photograph," and "biography."
exampleBody: You know the word "play." Now you see the word "playful." Even if you have never seen "playful" before, the root "play" gives you a big clue. Add the suffix "ful" meaning full of, and you know it means full of play, or fun and energetic!
exampleImage: A cartoon trail of footprints leading from the word "play" to "playful" to "playground" to "replay," showing how one root connects to many words along the path.
tipBody: When you see an unknown word, strip away any prefixes and suffixes to find the root. If the root looks familiar, you already have a clue about the word's meaning. Then add back the prefix and suffix meanings to figure out the whole word!
tipImage: A cartoon child peeling away layers of a word like an onion: the outer layer says "un," the middle layer says "break," and the inner layer says "able," revealing the root "break" at the core.
practiceBody: You now know how to use root words as clues! Find the root in each word and use what you know to figure out the meaning. You are a root word detective!
practiceImage: A cartoon student digging in a garden and pulling up root words from the ground, each root connected to a plant that has grown into a bigger word above the soil.

title: Using Glossaries and Dictionaries
introBody: Sometimes the best way to find out what a word means is to look it up! Today we will learn how to use glossaries and beginning dictionaries to find the precise meaning of words and phrases. These tools are like a word expert in your backpack!
introImage: A cartoon student opening a giant golden dictionary that glows with knowledge, with words and definitions floating out of the pages like magic.
teachHeading: Glossaries and Dictionaries Are Word Guides
teachBody: A glossary is found at the back of a book and lists important words from that book with their meanings. A dictionary lists thousands of words in alphabetical order with definitions, pronunciations, and sometimes example sentences. Both tools help you find the exact meaning of a word!
teachImage: Two cartoon books side by side: a textbook open to a glossary page in the back, and a thick dictionary with tabs for each letter of the alphabet, both glowing helpfully.
exampleBody: Imagine you are reading a science book and see the word "erosion." You flip to the glossary in the back and find: "Erosion: the process of wearing away soil and rock by wind, water, or ice." Now you know exactly what it means! The glossary had the answer.
exampleImage: A cartoon child flipping to the back of a science textbook, with a finger pointing to the glossary entry for "erosion" and a small diagram showing wind and water wearing away rock.
tipBody: Both glossaries and dictionaries are organized in alphabetical order. If you are looking for "planet," go to the P section. If two words start with the same letter, look at the second letter. This helps you find words quickly!
tipImage: A cartoon student running their finger down a dictionary page, passing words in alphabetical order, with colorful letter tabs on the side of the dictionary for quick navigation.
practiceBody: You now know how to use glossaries and dictionaries to find word meanings! Use these tools whenever you need to find the precise definition of a word. You are a research pro!
practiceImage: A cartoon student sitting at a desk with a dictionary and glossary open, writing down a definition on a notecard, with a neat stack of completed notecards nearby and a badge that says "Word Researcher."

title: Figurative Language and Word Relationships
introBody: Language is more than just facts and definitions. Words can create pictures in your mind, show how things are connected, and have different shades of meaning. Today we will explore figurative language, word relationships, and word nuances. Get ready for a word adventure!
introImage: A cartoon paintbrush dipping into a pot of colorful words and painting a vivid scene on a canvas, with phrases like "cold as ice" and "heart of gold" floating in the air.
teachBody: Figurative language uses words in creative ways to paint pictures or express feelings. Word relationships show how words connect to each other. And nuances are the small differences in meaning between similar words. Understanding these layers makes you a much stronger reader and writer!
teachImage: A cartoon layer cake with three layers: the bottom labeled "Literal Meaning," the middle labeled "Figurative Meaning," and the top labeled "Nuances," with frosting and a cherry on top.
exampleBody: "Her smile was sunshine." This is figurative language! Her smile is not actually the sun. It means her smile was warm and bright. Now think about "happy" vs. "thrilled." Both mean feeling good, but "thrilled" is much stronger. That difference is a nuance!
exampleImage: A cartoon girl with a radiant smile and golden sunshine rays beaming from her face, next to a thermometer of happiness showing "happy" at medium and "thrilled" at the very top.
tipBody: When you read a phrase that sounds strange if taken literally, it is probably figurative language. Ask yourself what picture or feeling the author is trying to create. And when you see similar words, think about what makes them slightly different from each other.
tipImage: A cartoon child looking at a sentence through special glasses: one lens shows the literal meaning and the other shows the figurative meaning, with the figurative side being more colorful.
practiceBody: You are ready to explore the layers of language! Read each passage and think about figurative language, word connections, and the subtle differences between similar words. You are a language artist!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing an artist's beret, standing in front of an easel where they have painted a colorful masterpiece made entirely of creative words and phrases.

title: Literal vs. Nonliteral Meanings
introHeading: Say What You Mean, or Do You?
introBody: Sometimes people say things that do not mean exactly what the words say. "I'm so hungry I could eat a horse!" does not mean you would actually eat a horse! Today we will learn to tell the difference between literal and nonliteral meanings in context.
introImage: A cartoon child looking shocked at a giant horse on a dinner plate, then laughing and realizing the person just means they are very hungry.
teachHeading: Literal Means Exactly, Nonliteral Means Creatively
teachBody: Literal meaning is the actual, word-for-word meaning. "The ice cream melted" is literal. Nonliteral meaning uses words creatively. "Time flies" does not mean time has wings! It means time passes quickly. The context around the phrase helps you figure out which meaning the author intends.
teachImage: Two cartoon clocks: one with tiny wings literally flying through the sky, crossed out with an X, and one next to a person looking surprised at how fast the day went, with a checkmark.
exampleBody: Read this: After running the race, Jake was on top of the world. Is Jake standing on the planet Earth? No! This is nonliteral. It means Jake felt amazing and proud after his race. The context of winning a race tells us it describes his happy feeling.
exampleImage: A cartoon boy standing triumphantly on top of a cartoon globe after a race, with a gold medal around his neck and fireworks in the background, showing the feeling of being on top of the world.
tipBody: If a phrase sounds strange or impossible when taken literally, it is probably nonliteral. Use the sentences around it to figure out what the author really means. The context is always your best clue for figuring out nonliteral language!
tipImage: A cartoon detective with a magnifying glass examining the words around a nonliteral phrase, with clue arrows pointing from the context to the true meaning.
practiceBody: You can now spot the difference between literal and nonliteral meanings! Read each passage and use context to figure out what the words really mean. You are a language detective!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing between two doors: one labeled "Literal" showing plain text, and one labeled "Nonliteral" showing creative colorful text, confidently choosing the right one.

title: Real-Life Word Connections
introBody: Words are not just things you read in books. They connect to your real life every day! Today we will learn how to identify real-life connections between words and how they are used. This will help you understand words on a deeper level!
introImage: A cartoon student surrounded by everyday objects: a cozy blanket, a pet dog, a soccer ball, and a lunchbox, with word labels floating above each one connected by dotted lines.
teachBody: Think about the word "cozy." You probably picture a warm blanket, a soft couch, or hot cocoa by a fireplace. That is a real-life connection! When you connect a word to something you have experienced, you understand it better and remember it longer.
teachImage: A cartoon web diagram with the word "cozy" in the center, connected to images of a warm fireplace, fuzzy slippers, a blanket fort, and a purring cat on a lap.
exampleBody: Think about the word "courageous." When have you been courageous in real life? Maybe you tried a new food, spoke up in class, or helped someone who was being teased. Those real experiences help you truly understand what "courageous" means!
exampleImage: A cartoon child standing up bravely to give a speech in front of their class, with a thought bubble connecting the word "courageous" to their real-life experience.
tipHeading: Ask: When Have I Seen or Felt This?
tipBody: When you learn a new word, ask yourself when you have experienced it in real life. Have you felt "frustrated"? Have you seen something "magnificent"? Connecting words to your own experiences makes them stick in your memory!
tipImage: A cartoon child lying in the grass looking at clouds, with each cloud shaped like a word and a memory attached: "excited" with a birthday party, "exhausted" with a long hike.
practiceBody: You now know how to connect words to real life! Think about your own experiences as you read each passage. Making connections will help you understand and remember new words. Let's go!
practiceImage: A cartoon student building a bridge made of words, connecting a book on one side to real-life scenes on the other side, with a sign that says "Word Connection Bridge."

title: Shades of Meaning
introHeading: Not All Words Are Created Equal!
introBody: Words that seem similar can actually have different strengths or shades of meaning. "Happy" and "ecstatic" both mean feeling good, but one is way stronger! Today we will learn to tell the difference between words that describe states of mind or degrees of certainty.
introImage: A cartoon paint palette with different shades of blue, from light to dark, and above each shade is a word: "content," "happy," "joyful," "thrilled," "ecstatic."
teachBody: Some words are mild and some are strong. "Dislike" is milder than "hate." "Possible" is less certain than "definite." Understanding these shades of meaning helps you choose the perfect word and understand exactly what an author means.
teachImage: A cartoon thermometer showing feelings from cold to hot: at the bottom is "annoyed" (cool blue), in the middle is "angry" (warm orange), and at the top is "furious" (blazing red).
exampleBody: Put these words in order from weakest to strongest: "furious," "annoyed," "angry." The answer is: annoyed, angry, furious! "Annoyed" is a little bothered, "angry" is more upset, and "furious" is extremely mad. They all describe being upset, but in different degrees!
exampleImage: Three cartoon faces in a row showing increasing levels of anger: a slightly frowning face labeled "annoyed," a clearly upset face labeled "angry," and a red-faced steaming face labeled "furious."
tipBody: When you see similar words, imagine them on a scale from weakest to strongest. Where does each word fall? "Sure" is stronger than "maybe" but weaker than "absolutely certain." Placing words on a mental scale helps you understand exactly how strong each one is.
tipImage: A cartoon balance scale with the word "maybe" on one side (light) and "absolutely certain" on the other side (heavy), with "probably" balanced in the middle.
practiceBody: You can now spot the shades of meaning between similar words! Read each passage and think about how strong or mild each word is. Choosing the right shade makes you a word expert!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing in front of a giant paint-by-numbers canvas, filling in sections with the perfect shade of meaning for each word, creating a beautiful completed picture.

title: Building Your Vocabulary
introBody: The more words you know, the better you can read, write, and talk about the world around you. Today we will practice using conversational, academic, and domain-specific words and phrases accurately. Let's build an amazing vocabulary!
introImage: A cartoon piggy bank shaped like a brain, with word coins labeled "analyze," "habitat," "summarize," and "therefore" being dropped in through the slot on top.
teachHeading: Three Types of Words to Learn
teachBody: Conversational words are the ones you use every day with friends and family. Academic words like "compare," "explain," and "describe" are used in school across all subjects. Domain-specific words like "ecosystem" belong to a particular subject. Learning all three types makes you a powerful communicator!
teachImage: A cartoon bookshelf with three labeled sections: "Everyday Words" with casual speech bubbles, "Academic Words" with a graduation cap, and "Domain-Specific Words" with a microscope and globe.
exampleBody: Instead of saying "The book was good," try "The book was fascinating!" Instead of "Tell me about it," try "Describe it to me." And in science, instead of "where an animal lives," say "its habitat." Using precise words makes your ideas clearer and more interesting!
exampleImage: A cartoon before-and-after scene: on the left, a plain sentence "The bug was cool" in gray. On the right, a sparkling sentence "The insect was remarkable" in gold, with a wow effect.
tipBody: Keep a personal word journal or word wall. Every time you learn a new word, write it down with its meaning and a sentence using it. Try to use at least one new word every day in your speaking or writing. Your vocabulary will grow fast!
tipImage: A cartoon student adding a new word card to a colorful word wall in their bedroom, with hundreds of words already posted, and a calendar showing a word-a-day streak.
practiceBody: You are ready to show off your growing vocabulary! Use the right words in the right places. Choose precise, accurate words that show exactly what you mean. You are a vocabulary superstar!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing on a winner's podium at a vocabulary contest, holding a trophy shaped like the letter V, with confetti raining down and a banner reading "Vocabulary Champion!"
